Name: kernel_fusion_for_memory_bound_ops
Title: Efficient Kernel Fusion for Memory-Bound Operations in Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: By strategically fusing memory-bound operations in transformer inference kernels, we can reduce memory bandwidth pressure and improve overall throughput, as measured by tokens-per-second on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Existing work on kernel fusion focuses on compute-bound operations or general-purpose optimizations (e.g., AMP-ViT, SNIP). However, memory-bound operations like attention softmax and layer normalization remain under-optimized. This proposal specifically targets these operations, leveraging CUDA/Triton to fuse them with adjacent compute-bound kernels, reducing HBM round-trips and improving hardware utilization.
Abstract: Transformer models dominate modern AI workloads, but their inference efficiency is often bottlenecked by memory-bound operations such as softmax and layer normalization. While kernel fusion has been explored for compute-bound tasks, memory-bound operations remain a challenge due to their dependency on high-bandwidth memory (HBM) accesses. This paper proposes a novel approach to fuse memory-bound operations with adjacent compute-bound kernels, reducing HBM round-trips and improving overall throughput. We design CUDA/Triton kernels that integrate softmax and layer normalization with matrix multiplications, optimizing for coalesced memory accesses and tensor-core occupancy. Our experiments on commodity GPUs demonstrate significant improvements in tokens-per-second and reduced latency, validated by nvprof/Nsight traces showing reduced warp stalls and improved bandwidth utilization. This work bridges the gap between memory-bound and compute-bound optimizations, offering practical speedups for transformer inference.
Experiments: Baseline profiling: Measure latency and bandwidth utilization of unfused softmax and layer normalization kernels using nvprof/Nsight.
Fused kernel implementation: Develop CUDA/Triton kernels that fuse softmax and layer normalization with matrix multiplications, optimizing for coalesced loads and tensor-core occupancy.
A/B latency comparison: Compare fused and unfused kernels on transformer inference tasks, reporting p50/p99 latency, tokens-per-second, and energy per token.
Hardware-level validation: Use nvprof/Nsight to measure warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity for fused kernels.
Generalization test: Evaluate fused kernels across different transformer architectures (e.g., ViT, GPT, BERT) and sequence lengths.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Fusion may increase register pressure, leading to register spilling and reduced performance.
The benefits of fusion may diminish for small batch sizes or short sequence lengths.
Implementation complexity may limit portability across GPU architectures.
Fused kernels may introduce numerical instability in edge cases.
